summary:
Porcelain typically does not contain calcium carbonate as a primary component; this substance may be used occasionally but is generally absent from standard formulations due to well-established historical practices and compositions. The absence reflects adherence to specific criteria defining true porcelain material properties. Calcium carbonate can sometimes appear in special cases outside these norms, though such instances are uncommon within typical industrial applications involving classic ceramic materials like porcelain. To directly answer your query succinctly while focusing strictly on core elements pertinent here,

Calcium carbonate is notably excluded from regular porcelain production according to recognized standards despite potential occasional use under unique circumstances beyond common practice. This exclusion underscores fidelity towards authentic porcelain characteristics defined by tradition rather than including extraneous components unless absolutely necessary for particular purposes
